title: To Cool, or Not to Cool? Displacement Sensing with Hot Quantum States
abstract: Quantum-enhanced displacement sensing with bosonic systems is typically formulated assuming that the oscillator is cooled close to its ground state before nonclassical probe preparation. We investigate whether such near-ground-state initialization is necessary, or whether sensitive probes can instead be generated directly from thermal states. We analyze hot quantum probes produced by squeezing, number-raising, and Schrödinger-cat-state generation applied to thermal inputs. We identify two distinct mechanisms by which thermal mixedness can remain compatible with enhanced displacement sensitivity. First, projecting a mixed probe onto a definite parity sector removes the usual thermal suppression of the displacement quantum Fisher information, which can then increase with initial thermal occupation. Second, coherent superpositions of opposite displacements can retain sensitivity through coherence between their displaced components, even when the underlying state is mixed. We use these two mechanisms to classify hot-state protocols according to whether their sensitivity comes from parity selection, coherence between displaced components, or both. Finally, we formulate an experimentally relevant optimization problem comparing initial cooling with direct hot-state preparation under realistic decoherence and show that complete cooling is not universally optimal. Our results establish hot-state engineering as a route to quantum-enhanced bosonic displacement sensing without mandatory ground-state initialization.
summary: The paper challenges the assumption that quantum-enhanced bosonic sensing requires cooling to the ground state. By analyzing hot quantum states generated via squeezing or catification, it identifies two mechanisms—parity filtering and coherent branch interference—that maintain high sensitivity even with thermal noise. Ultimately, it shows that optimizing for the rate of information gain over time favors direct preparation of structured hot states over extensive pre-cooling.
main_problem: The study investigates whether quantum-enhanced displacement sensing using bosonic systems requires mandatory cooling to near the ground state, or if high sensitivity can be achieved directly from thermal (hot) states.
main_result: Hot-state engineering protocols—specifically those utilizing parity selection or coherent branch interference—can achieve quantum enhancement without requiring full ground-state initialization. Optimal sensing depends on maximizing coherence per unit total experimental time, not just single-shot QFI.
method: The analysis involves deriving the Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) for various hot states and comparing the scaling of this resource under different decoherence channels (loss, heating, dephasing).
model_or_system: Bosonic systems are modeled using master equations incorporating bosonic loss, motional heating, and number dephasing. Specific protocols analyzed include parity-filtered cat states (KPO/qcMAP) and Echoed Conditional Displacement (ECD) cat states.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), $\Delta	ilde{\epsilon}$, noise susceptibility ($\dot{F}$), and the rate objective $R = F / t_{cycle}$.
important_parameters: Initial thermal occupation ($n_{th}$), squeezing parameter ($r$), decoherence rates ($\Gamma_h, \gamma_-, \Gamma_\phi$), and protocol preparation time ($	au_d$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often relies on effective two-branch models or approximations for the rate objective $R$, assuming certain overhead times are negligible or fixed.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate operational cycles, scaling laws for different mechanisms (parity filtering vs. branch separation), and normalized QFI dynamics under various noise channels (loss, diffusion, dephasing).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from identifying the core problem to analyzing two key enhancement mechanisms (Parity Filtering and Coherent Interference). It then rigorously compares these hot-state protocols across different decoherence models, culminating in an optimization comparison between cooling overhead versus direct preparation.
why_interesting: This work provides a crucial resource for quantum sensing by shifting the focus from achieving perfect initial state purity to engineering specific coherence structures within mixed thermal states, which is highly relevant for practical implementations in trapped ions or cavity QED.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 2. Optimization of cooling and driving times for trapped-ion hot-state sensing using full numerical master-equation simulations. (a) Full QFI as a function of cooling time τc and driving time τd. (b) Effective metrological gain per unit preparation time, F/(τc + τd), showing the emergence of an optimal finite driving time despite the monotonic increase of F. The calculations assume initial thermal occupation nth = 10, cooling rate γc = 1, motional-heating rate γh = 10−3, and ancilla-spin-dephasing rate γs = 10−2, correspond- ing to experimentally realistic trapped-ion parameters.
